output_text:
Summary: Rewrote the section to tighten claims, fix a technical inaccuracy, foreground the contribution, and flag two issues you should resolve before publication.

## Recommendations

1. **Technical inaccuracy in paragraph 2.** "This marginalization assumes that the latent variable's posterior is conjugate to the prior under the observation model" is muddled. Conjugacy is a property of a prior–likelihood pair (the posterior is in the same family as the prior); a posterior is not "conjugate to the prior." More importantly, marginalizing out a *discrete* latent typically does not require conjugacy at all — it requires that the discrete variable has finite support so the sum is tractable, and that downstream variables remain tractable after summation. Conjugacy is the condition for marginalizing out *continuous* latents analytically. Decide which case you actually mean and rewrite accordingly. As written, a reviewer with PGM background will flag this.

2. **"Order of magnitude" vs. measured 3.4× median.** Paragraph 2 claims a 10× reduction; paragraph 4 reports median 3.4×. Either qualify the analytical claim ("asymptotic cost in the cardinality of the latent's support") or drop it.

3. **Silent-skip behavior is a real usability issue.** You note in paragraph 5 that users aren't warned when the transformation is skipped. Don't bury this — either fix it (emit a diagnostic) or justify it. Reviewers will ask.

4. **Paragraph 3 is filler.** "Many practitioners write models with discrete latents anyway and might benefit" adds nothing. Cut or replace with a concrete motivating example.

5. **Forward reference to Section 4 is fine but redundant with paragraph 3.** Consolidate.

6. **"Main technical contribution" hedging.** "which we believe represents" is weak. State it.

## Revised text

> We present a probabilistic programming interface that compiles models written in a high-level DSL into efficient inference code. The compiler's main contribution is an automatic marginalization pass that rewrites discrete latent variables out of the model whenever static analysis can prove the rewrite is sound.
>
> Given a discrete latent variable with finite support, the pass replaces sampling with explicit summation over its values, propagating the resulting mixture through downstream factors. The rewrite is sound when (i) the latent's support is bounded and known at compile time, and (ii) downstream dependencies admit closed-form summation — in particular, when the latent's children are conjugate to its prior. Both conditions are checked by the static analysis described in Section 4; when either fails, the pass is skipped and the compiler emits a diagnostic identifying the blocking dependency.
>
> On a benchmark of 23 user-written models, the pass applied to 17. Measured end-to-end inference speedups ranged from 1.2× to 18×, with a median of 3.4×. The asymptotic cost reduction scales with the cardinality of the marginalized variable's support, which accounts for the spread.

## Uncertainty

I'm assuming your transformation is the standard sum-out / variable-elimination style marginalization for discrete latents; if it's actually a continuous-latent conjugacy collapse (e.g., Beta-Binomial, Normal-Normal), the conjugacy framing in your original was closer to correct and my revision needs to be flipped accordingly. Clarify which you mean and adjust paragraph 2 of the revision.
